meta.coverage: All major cultural regions: Valle de México, Veracruz/Nahua Sierra de Zongolica, Yucatán/Maya Peninsula, Bajío/Jalisco, Mexico national

sources.title: Compendio de plantas medicinales silvestres de la región nahua de la Sierra de Zongolica, Veracruz
sources.author: Victoria de Jesús Francisca
sources.publisher: Instituto Nacional de los Pueblos Indígenas (INPI)

sources.title: Guía Ilustrada de Plantas Medicinales en el Valle de México
sources.author: Fonseca Chávez, Rivera Levario & Vázquez García
sources.publisher: Instituto Nacional de los Pueblos Indígenas (INPI)
sources.region: Valle de México — multi-ethnic (Nahua, Otomí, Mazahua, Purépecha, Totonaca, Tenek)

sources.title: Plantas Medicinales: La Realidad de una Tradición Ancestral
sources.author: Guzmán Maldonado, Díaz Huacuz & González Chavira
sources.publisher: INIFAP — Campo Experimental Bajío, Celaya, Guanajuato
sources.region: Bajío / Guanajuato — scientific review of 8 commonly sold species

sources.title: Medicina Experimentada — Plantas Sanadoras
sources.author: Fernández Martínez & Gutiérrez
sources.publisher: Instituto Nacional de los Pueblos Indígenas (INPI)

sources.title: Catálogo de la Colección de Plantas Medicinales del Jardín X ka'ansajil (Lugar de enseñanza)
sources.author: Méndez-González, Pacheco-Garrido, Torrez-Avilez, Dorantes-Euán & Durán-García; medicinal knowledge: Édgar Francisco Peraza Chan
sources.publisher: CONACYT-FORDECYT — Jardín Botánico Regional Roger Orellana, CICY

sources.title: Plantas Medicinales de la Farmacia Viviente del CEFOFOR: usos terapéuticos tradicionales y dosificación
sources.author: CONAFOR / Gerencia de Educación y Capacitación
sources.region: Ciudad Guzmán, Jalisco — national herbolaria with dosage protocols

plants.notes: CRITICAL: Mexican árnica ≠ European Arnica montana. Different sesquiterpene profile. Contains chamissonolida and helenalin derivatives but at different concentrations. Widely confused at point of sale.
European species; documented in Mexican institutional pharmacopeia but rarely available as authentic plant. Often replaced by Heterotheca inuloides or Tithonia diversifolia in commerce.
Called 'árnica' in Yucatan and Honduras. Part of the documented Árnica triangle across the dataset. No equivalent helenalin content to true Arnica montana.

Syn: Chenopodium ambrosioides. Most widely documented species across all 6 sources. Ascaridol content — TOXIC in excess. Clinical evidence for antiparasitic activity against Trypanosoma, Plasmodium, Leishmania in animal studies.
Most widely used aromatic herb in Mexico. GRAS status (FDA). Clinical evidence for antibacterial activity.
Distinct from M. spicata though both sold as 'hierbabuena'. Higher menthol content. Sometimes listed as 'hierbabuena de menta'.
Third 'hierbabuena' variant. Called 'poleo' in Estado de México — ambiguity with Mentha pulegium (true poleo).
True poleo. Distinct pharmacology from M.spicata/viridis. Slightly toxic; abortifacient properties documented. Name 'poleo' also applied to M.viridis in MX — ambiguity risk.
Contains thujone — toxic in excess. Not for use in pregnancy. Documented in Zongolica, Valle de MX, INPI 2021, and CEFOFOR 2010.

plants.scientific_name: Artemisia ludoviciana Nutt. subsp. mexicana (Willd.) Keck
plants.notes: Mexican native 'ajenjo'. Distinct from A. absinthium. Multiple indigenous names across Nahua, Otomí, Zapotec, Tenek. Abortifacient use documented.

Most universally documented species across all 6 sources. GRAS. Scientific evidence for wound healing, antifungal, antibacterial, anti-inflammatory. Also called 'sávila', 'zabila'. Náhuatl: posacmetl. Maya: hunpets'k'inki.
Widely used for digestive issues and ritual cleansing ('limpias', 'mal de ojo'). Maya: TRAMIL-validated for mal de ojo baths. Narcotic in high doses — not for children under 2 or elderly.
Maya Yucatan species distinct from O. basilicum. TRAMIL-validated for wound/sore healing. Not widely known outside Maya region.
Silymarin active compound — hepatoprotective, documented clinically. Best administered in capsules (loses properties in water per INPI 2021). Widely used across all MX regions.

TOXIC — protopine, berberine, isoquinolines. TRAMIL validates only baths for skin eruptions; NOT oral ingestion. Zongolica uses latex externally on warts. S6 warns: do not exceed 10-day oral use. Ambiguity: called 'cardo santo' in Zongolica but Cirsium subcoriaceum is also 'cardo santo' in Jalisco.
Distinct from A. mexicana (yellow flowers vs white-yellowish). Valle de México. Uses parallel A. mexicana but less documented.

plants.common_name_primary: Chicalote de montaña / Cardo santo blanco
plants.notes: Mexican native. Source S2 flags as TÓXICA. Flujo vaginal, riñones, diabetes uses documented. Toxic alkaloids as per all Argemone spp.

CRITICAL AMBIGUITY: called 'cardo santo' in Jalisco/Bajío region but is a completely different plant from Argemone mexicana (also 'cardo santo'). Cirsium subcoriaceum is Asteraceae; A. mexicana is Papaveraceae. Uses: hepatic, asthma, cardiac conditions.

plants.scientific_name: Taraxacum officinale G. H. Weber ex Wigg.
plants.notes: Widely naturalized in Mexico. Used across all regions for diabetes, diuretic, liver, constipation. Rich in inulin and flavonoids.
Used for renal conditions across all sources. High silica content. Do not use excessively — leaches minerals.
Closely related to E. arvense; Valle de México source distinguishes the two. Hollow stems characteristic. Used also for renal and digestive conditions.
Náhuatl: Xalxocotl. TRAMIL-validated for diarrhea (leaves). Also antispasmodic, antibacterial. Widely used postpartum baths. One of the best-documented Mexican medicinal plants.

plants.common_name_primary: Hierba del cáncer / Hierba del golpe
plants.notes: Same species, different common names in different sources: S1 Zongolica distinguishes 'Hierba del cáncer' (flowers/leaves infusion for cancer/tumors) from 'Hierba del golpe' (pelvic issues, women only). S2 and S4 unify both uses. Náhuatl names differ: Chinawaxiwitl (cancer), Koskatlakuatzin (golpe).
Náhuatl: Cacahuapaxtle / Siwapahxiwitl. Antiseptic, astringent, cicatrizant. Diarrhea, dysentry, menstrual disorders. Well-documented from Zongolica to Valle de México. Also in Honduras dataset.
Náhuatl: Sempualxóchitl. Also: Apátsicua (Purépecha), cempoalxóchitl (Náhuatl). Day of the Dead flower. Medicinal: digestive, antiparasitic, antispasmodic. Natural dye. Widely documented.
Náhuatl: yauhtli. Huichol ritual use (mixed with Nicotiana rustica). Abortive and emenagogue use documented (S2). Digestive, antispasmodic. Distinct from T. micrantha and T. erecta.
Anise-scented. Smaller than T. lucida. Digestive, antiparasitic. Valle de México.

TOXIC (moderate): abortifacient, emenagogue. Not for pregnant women or children. Despite toxicity, widely used across all regions for menstrual disorders, ritual cleansing (mal de ojo, espanto), digestive complaints. Rutaceae compounds. TRAMIL validated (Yucatan) for headache/mal de ojo baths at specific doses.
Often used interchangeably with R. graveolens in Mexico. Pharmacologically similar. S6 lists both R. chalepensis and R. graveolens as 'ruda'. Treated as one entry in commerce.

plants.common_name_primary: Muicle / Hierba azul / Sangre de Cristo
plants.notes: Náhuatl: mouait/mouitl. Used prehispanically. Blood purifier, anemia, menstrual disorders, dengue. In Yucatan: 'siete vidas/pico de colibrí' — for liver problems. Cross-dataset: also in NI (Insulina crisis) — NOT the same use here. Here it is NOT called insulina in MX sources.

Widely used culinary herb (tamales, salsas) AND medicinal. Safrole content — not for extended internal use. TRAMIL (NI) lists as orally toxic. MX sources use extensively for fever, digestive issues. Critical: also called 'hoja santa' creating confusion with Hoja Santa (Piper sanctus = synonym).

S6 treats as distinct from Piper auritum. Current taxonomy considers P. sanctus a synonym of P. auritum. Uses: cough, bronchitis, asthma, muscle pain. Note from S4: safrole — not more than 15 days continuous use.

plants.scientific_name: Lippia alba (Mill.) N.E. Br. ex Britton & P. Wilson
plants.notes: Zongolica/Veracruz: used for diarrhea, digestive disorders, tachycardia. Cross-dataset conflict: Lippia alba = 'Mastranto' in CR/PA but different common name in MX. Zongolica calls it 'hierba de tapón' — unique regional name.
Multiple Verbena-named plants across MX dataset. This is V. officinalis (European). Distinct from Stachytarpheta jamaicensis called 'verbena' in Yucatan, and from V. bipinnatifida (S2).
Valle de México. Called 'moradilla' or 'alfombrilla de campo'. Used for fever in children. Different from V. officinalis. Distinct from Stachytarpheta jamaicensis ('verbena' in Yucatan).

CRITICAL AMBIGUITY: called 'verbena' in Yucatan but is a completely different plant from Verbena officinalis (European) and Verbena bipinnatifida. Used to regulate menstruation. FloraLexicon must flag the Verbena quadruplet: V. officinalis / V. bipinnatifida / Stachytarpheta jamaicensis / Hyptis verticillata (Guatemala dataset).

Syn: C. pictus D. Don, C. mexicanus. Scientific evidence (S3 INIFAP): reduces blood glucose in diabetic animal models; one human clinical study confirms glucose reduction. Leaves contain flavonoids, steroids, triterpenoids. Zongolica community uses as 'insulina natural'. EXTENDS the Insulina crisis from NI — here the MX species has clinical backing, unlike NI alternatives.

Zongolica community calls this 'insulina natural' for diabetes, creating local cross-confusion with Costus igneus. Also called 'falso boldo', 'buscapina natural'. Different genus from true Boldo (Peumus boldus, Monimiaceae). Eucalyptus-like essential oils; sedative properties also documented.

TRAMIL-validated in Yucatan for lowering blood sugar and cholesterol. Called 'orégano de castilla' in Yucatan — DISTINCT from Origanum vulgare (true oregano) and from Lippia graveolens (orégano CR). This is the same genus as the Mexican 'boldo mexicano' (Plectranthus ornatus). Three plants in two genera sold as 'orégano' in MX.

True Chilean boldo. Listed in INPI 2021 compendium. Biliary dysfunction, diuretic, digestive. Chronic use irritates gastric mucosa. Distinct from Mexican 'falso boldo' (Plectranthus ornatus). South American origin — cross-dataset: appears in Bolivia/Argentina datasets.

INIFAP clinical review: steviósidos are 143-300x sweeter than sugar. Clinical trials in diabetic patients reduce fasting glucose, improve post-prandial glucose. No teratogenic, mutagenic, or carcinogenic effects in studies. Guaraní origin — in commercial cultivation worldwide including Mexico.

INIFAP clinical review: penetrates deep skin layers; anti-inflammatory, anxiolytic. 120+ chemical compounds including apigenin, chamazulene. Clinical evidence for colic in infants, eczema, GI disorders, anxiety, diabetes. GRAS (FDA). Most consumed herbal tea worldwide.

Distinct from Matricaria chamomilla (German chamomile). S4 notes that Manzanilla romana 'conserves some properties of manzanilla dulce, though should not be used the same way' — pharmacological distinction important. Contains nobilin instead of chamazulene.

plants.common_name_primary: Sauco / Flor y fruto de sauco
plants.notes: Náhuatl: Xometl. Used in Zongolica for respiratory conditions, ritual cleansing, and fever. S4 lists comprehensively: antiviral, antipyretic, diuretic, anti-catarrhal. Fruits TOXIC raw. CRITICAL: S4 notes 'los frutos son tóxicos' — raw elder berries cause GI distress.

plants.scientific_name: Sambucus mexicana C. Presl ex DC.
plants.notes: Mexican elder, distinct from S. nigra. Valle de México — multiple indigenous names across Tzeltal, Tzotzil, Otomí, Totonaca, Tenek, Náhuatl. Uses largely parallel S. nigra. FloraLexicon must distinguish: European vs Mexican species sold as 'sauco'.

HIGH TOXICITY: seeds contain ricin — potentially lethal. Documented across NI, HN, GT datasets. MX sources (S2,S5,S6) use LEAVES externally only (fever, bilis, empacho, hemorrhoids). TRAMIL-validated in Yucatan for hair loss and fever using leaves. S6 explicitly notes: 'la semilla es ligeramente tóxica' — UNDERSTATEMENT: ricin is among the most toxic natural substances. CRITICAL NAME COLLISION: 'higuera/higuerilla' vs Ficus carica (edible fig, also 'higuera' in S4).

Edible fig. S4 lists for bronchopulmonary diseases, constipation, diuretic. CRITICAL DISAMBIGUATION: 'higuera' in S4 = edible fig with NO toxicity. 'Higuerilla' or 'higuera del diablo' in S2/S5/S6 = Ricinus communis (LETHAL ricin). The danger: in commerce, abbreviated names cause confusion. FloraLexicon must flag this as highest-risk collision in MX dataset.

HIGH TOXICITY: tropane alkaloids (atropine, scopolamine, hyoscyamine). S2 documents ritual/spiritual use (Huichol — 'kieri'; Yaqui — 'veneno divino'). S6 recommends ONLY external use (sitz baths for hemorrhoids, muscle pain). Hallucinogen. Criminal use documented (involuntary sedation). NOT for internal use under any circumstances.

HIGH TOXICITY: vinca alkaloids (vincristine, vinblastine) — chemotherapy agents, toxic to normal cells. TRAMIL recommends AVOIDING oral ingestion entirely. S5 (Yucatan/TRAMIL) lists conjunctivitis use of white flower infusion — ophthalmically. S6 lists as 'Vinca rosea' for allergies and cancer prevention (infusion) — CONTRADICTS TRAMIL safety guidance. Must flag S6 use as dangerous.

HIGH TOXICITY: seeds LETHAL. TRAMIL validates ONLY latex application for oral candidiasis. S5 also lists for diarrhea (oral route with leaves) — TRAMIL validates this use. Seeds must NEVER be ingested. Cross-dataset: 'piñón' collision with edible pine nuts and Caramo (GT). Yucatan Maya name sikilte' is distinct.
Endemic to Yucatan Peninsula. Topical use only — fogajes (canker sores) and oral wounds. Closely related to J. curcas; assume similar toxicity for seeds. Poorly known outside Maya region.

HIGH TOXICITY: cardenolides (cardiac glycosides). S5 documents it used in complex multi-plant preparations for hemorrhoids (anal application) and rheumatism (topical). The fact that it is used alongside 12+ other plants suggests the community is aware of its danger. FloraLexicon must flag: NEVER use internally; cardiac glycoside toxicity.

Seeds contain casimiroína — potent hypnotic alkaloid. Flesh is safe. S4 and S6 use leaves for hypertension, insomnia, inflammation, headache. Cross-dataset: already flagged in HN dataset. Confirmation that this is a Mexico-wide risk: hypnotic alkaloid in seeds vs safe pulp.

CRITICAL DISAMBIGUATION (extends CR dataset): Tilo in MX/INPI 2021 = Tilia sp. (European linden, Malvaceae) — sedative, antispasmodic, anxiolytic. Documented flavonoids and sedative properties. Tilo in Costa Rica/CA = Justicia pectoralis (Acanthaceae) — NO sedative alkaloids. A person in Mexico seeking 'tilo' gets a pharmacologically different plant than one in CR. Must be flagged as inter-regional collision.

True sedative valeriana with documented anxiolytic valerenic acid. INPI 2021 uses for insomnia, anxiety, muscle cramps. EXTENDS the Valeriana crisis: V. officinalis (S4) has documented sedatives, V. sobifolia/edulis (S6) is Mexican species with different alkaloids, and Chrysopogon zizanioides (NI) has NO sedatives. Three plants sold as 'valeriana' with completely different pharmacology.

plants.scientific_name: Valeriana sobifolia Willd. / V. edulis Nutt.
plants.notes: Mexican native valeriana species. Hidalgo, Puebla, Valle de México. CEFOFOR documents same uses as V. officinalis (insomnia, migraine, anxiety). Isovaleric acid and valerenic acid profile differs from V. officinalis. May have sedative properties but degree unknown vs European species.

Also listed as 'Lantana sp.' in Zongolica (S1) as '5 negritos'. Multiple indigenous names across Náhuatl, Tzeltal, Tzotzil, Maya, Tenek. Digestive, respiratory, menstrual, topical uses. In Quechua (Peru): 'asna jora'. Cross-dataset relevance: appears across SA datasets too.

Flowers used for respiratory conditions (cough, asthma, bronchitis). Zongolica also uses for children's epilepsy. CEFOFOR: 3g per liter water every 4 hours during acute episode. Uses consistent across all MX sources.

Náhuatl: none. Maya: tuch xiiw. TRAMIL-validated (Yucatan) for hair loss, neonatal umbilicus healing, wound healing. Not for pregnant women or prostatitis patients (S4). Comprehensive postpartum baths use in Yucatan.

Zongolica only. Community was not previously familiar with it (S1: 'la comunidad no lo conocía'). External use for fracture consolidation, eczema, bruises. Contains allantoin (cicatrizant) and mucilages. Pyrrolizidine alkaloids — not for internal use long-term.

Widely used for blood pressure, cholesterol, diuretic, digestive. Tenek: jiliy tom. CROSS-DATASET: extends from NI, GT, HN, PA datasets. In NI it was documented as 'valeriana' in some commercial contexts — clarified here as lemongrass.
Náhuatl: Kakapaxiwitl. Sedative, anxiolytic. S4 warns: can create dependency. Used for menopause, palpitations, Parkinson tremors (S1), insomnia, anxiety, hypertension. Edible fruit.
Distinct from P. edulis. Yucatan Maya: vaginal lavages for breast cancer symptoms. Weedy vine; fruit edible but foetid.

Zongolica. Used for kidney stones — validated ethnobotanically. Also hepatoprotective, diuretic, antispasmodic. Pan-tropical use — important for cross-dataset integration (also in Bolivia, Guianas, Panama datasets).

plants.common_name_primary: Cola de León / Cardo / Flor de mundo
plants.notes: Naturalized in tropical America. Zongolica: antidiabetic and anti-inflammatory (leaves). S1: known as 'Varita de San José' in community. S2 documents comprehensive uses including gonorrhea, cataracts, rheumatism, female sterility. Good antidiabetic data.
Náhuatl: Tlalkahkaya. Zongolica: astringent, antiseptic, cicatrizant. Leaves edible (salads). Ointment for wound healing, insect stings, hemorrhoids. Nutritionally rich in vitamins A, C, calcium.

Zongolica: for dysentery, diarrhea, hemorrhoids. ABORTIVE properties (S1 explicitly lists it). CRITICAL CROSS-DATASET: T. zebrina = 'Hoja de Milagro' in CR (also abortive noted in GT dataset — 'no usar más de 15 días'). Different name in Zongolica but same safety risk.

Distinct from T. zebrina. TRAMIL-validated (Yucatan) for vaginal cleansing, pasmo, cough, menstrual delay, sprains/trauma. Widely used medicinally in Yucatan. Not to be confused with T. zebrina (also called 'maguey' in some regions).
Náhuatl: Pitzawakikitl. Zongolica: skin conditions, liver, herpes zoster, hemorrhoids. Edible as quelite (S1) — community eats it as greens. S2 (as S. americanum) also notes edibility with caution.
Distinct from S. nigrum though closely related. Valle de México: skin burns, sarampión, digestive. S2 notes ambiguity: 'some say edible, others say slightly toxic.' Fruits potentially toxic.

Zongolica: antiviral, antihypertensive, anticancer uses documented. Vaginal baths postpartum. Male reproductive system infections. Community doesn't use it much — calls it 'espina' (thorn plant). Source notes community barely knows it.

S1 Zongolica: used for prostatitis, psoriasis, diuretic, blood purifier. Community considers it a weed. S5 Yucatan: 'frijolillo' — TRAMIL-validated for respiratory conditions (asthma, bronchitis, cough). Multiple other regional names: hierba hedionda, cafecillo, cimarrón. Cross-dataset: also in NI, GT.
Náhuatl: iztakxochitl. Zongolica community uses to feed farmyard animals primarily. Anti-inflammatory, antidiabetic, expectorant, antifungal properties documented. AIDS mentioned in community use.
CEFOFOR: kidney stones, migraine, skin allergies, bladder problems, antibiotic. Jalisco/national use. Distinct from B. aurea (also called 'aceitilla' in MX and Central America).
See MX003. Documenting here as secondary entry with Yucatan-specific Maya name 'chiople' — used as complement in multi-plant preparations. Yucatan uses: stomach pain, rheumatism, diabetes.
Zongolica: sedative, analgesic, anti-inflammatory. Menstrual cramps. Hemorrhoids. Community uses for hemorrhoids primarily. Pyrrolizidine alkaloids possible — not for chronic internal use.

Náhuatl: Xochitopapanpapanxochitl. Zongolica: children's drooling, cough. Flowers in honey infusion. Respiratory conditions including tuberculosis, dysentery, nosebleeds, tonsillitis, stomach pain, insect stings.

plants.common_name_primary: Espada del rey / Lengua de tigre
plants.notes: Zongolica: extremely broad traditional uses listed including cancer, infections, diabetes, herpes, hepatitis, HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis, malaria. S1 appears to document community beliefs comprehensively. Note: scientific evidence for many of these indications is limited or absent. FloraLexicon should flag evidence_level as traditional for all uses.

plants.common_name_primary: Cabello de ángel / Tripa de judas / Kukustli (Náhuatl)
plants.notes: Parasitic vine. Náhuatl: Kukustli. Zongolica: headache, itching, amnesia, constipation, anticonvulsant, antioxidant, antihypertensive, anticancer, anthelmintic. Promotes hair growth including during chemotherapy. Some use for impotence and vertigo.
Zongolica: anti-inflammatory, glycemic control, cardiac protective, antioxidant, respiratory conditions, hair loss. Eaten as quelite in Hilicotla Magdalena community. Native weed.

plants.scientific_name: Pseudognaphalium obtusifolium (L.) Hilliard & B. L. Burtt
plants.notes: GORDOLOBO DISAMBIGUATION: This is the Zongolica/Nahua gordolobo. See also Gnaphalium oxyphyllum (Valle de México, S2) and Verbascum thapsus (European mullein, S4). Three different plants all called 'gordolobo' in Mexico. None are pharmacologically equivalent.
Valle de México gordolobo. Respiratory, digestive, skin uses. Closely related to Pseudognaphalium obtusifolium but distinct species. See MX081 for disambiguation context.

European mullein — listed as 'gordolobo' in the INPI 2021 compendium. Documented seeds toxicity (S4: 'las flores no tienen toxicidad, las semillas sí'). Respiratory conditions. THIRD 'gordolobo' in Mexico — completely different family from the two Mexican ones.

Common nettle. Zongolica, Valle de México, and Jalisco sources all document. Minerals, antirheumatic, diuretic, hair loss prevention. Benign prostatic hyperplasia (root). Stinging hairs cause contact urticaria.
Valle de México: muscle pain, measles baths, dandruff, anemia, asthma, nosebleeds, diarrhea. Native to Central America. Distinct from U. dioica.

Called 'ortiga' in Yucatan but distinct from Urtica dioica. Topical use for rheumatism. The three 'ortiga' plants in MX (U. dioica, U. chamaedryoides, Urera baccifera) represent another disambiguation challenge.

ABORTIFACIENT — documented explicitly. Náhuatl: zoapatli/cihuapatli meaning 'medicine of the woman'. Used to stimulate labor contractions, abortifacient, menstrual regulator. S2 explicitly notes: 'to abort: effective but dangerous.' Not to be used without medical supervision. Postpartum use (with epazote and ruda). Valley of Mexico.

Valle de México: exclusively gynecological uses. Parto, hemorragia vaginal, detener la menstruación, dolor de cadera. Otomí name: datha nejexmho. Purépecha: chun chinaskua. Distinct from European Betonica officinalis.

plants.common_name_primary: Maravilla / Don Diego de noche / Linda tarde
plants.notes: S2 comprehensive Valle de México uses: anti-inflammatory, vaginal infections, epilepsy, tumors. Root edible, also used in cooking (S2). S6 root for joint inflammation (topical, pomada). Flowers (S2) for epilepsy prevention.

Náhuatl: peloncuáhuitl. Zapoteca: yaga-ica. Extensively used ritually for 'mal de ojo', 'brujería', cleansings (limpias). Medical uses: rheumatism, venereals, arthritis. Cross-dataset: Schinus molle also appears in Bolivia/Argentina datasets.

Multiple indigenous names. Postpartum baths (widely documented across sources). Skin abscesses, fever, analgesic, hypnotic. S4 notes hypnotic properties — actual sedative alkaloids. Cross-dataset: Buddleia americana = 'salvia' in NI. Distinct species.
S2 Valle de México: gastrointestinal, skin, diabetes, venereal disease. S5 Yucatan (TRAMIL-validated): accelerates labor. Cross-dataset: also in CR, PA, GT.

Wild chaya. Yucatan: used with other plants (chintok, e'lemuy) for kidney stones. S4 INPI: same uses as cultivated chaya for diabetes and laxative. Distinct from C. chayamansa (cultivated chaya). Cross-dataset: also in HN dataset.

Cultivated chaya — distinct from C. aconitifolius (wild). S4 and S6 both document for diabetes and laxative. Rich in iron, calcium, vitamins. Leaves must be cooked (raw leaves of some varieties have urticating hairs and HCN compounds).
Valle de México. 'Mala mujer' = bad woman (stinging hairs). Used topically for toothache, rheumatism, ovarian inflammation, hypertension, snakebite, diabetes. Latex applied directly to painful tooth.

TOXIC: fruit potentially toxic per S2. Root used for polycystic breast (S6), skin dermatitis and fungal infections. S2: also anticonceptive, diuretic, weight loss. S6: 'planta tóxica'. Fruits must NOT be eaten.
TOXIC: latex applied topically only for warts, calluses, mezquinos. Chihuahuan Desert endemic. Wax-producing plant of major commercial importance (candelilla wax). S6 clearly states: TÓXICA.
Poinsettia — iconic Mexican ornamental. S6 documents for vaginal hemorrhage and menstrual regulation (4g petals per liter, 10 days). Latex irritating. Christmas symbol worldwide.
Mexico's national tree. Grows along rivers. Bark and leaves for hypertension, hemorrhoids, varicose veins. Strong cocimiento.
Mexican endemic genus. Flowers for acne, dermatosis. Bulbs as hormonal precursors. Jalisco/national use.

Mexican endemic sage. Jalisco/Bajío. Note: called 'mastranzo' in CEFOFOR which creates ambiguity — 'mastranzo' is also used for Marrubium vulgare (S4) and Mentha sylvestris in SA contexts. Sedative, headache, bilious pain.
European sage. S4: hormonal precursor, menstrual regulation, cicatrizant, inhibits sweating. S6: menstrual regulator. Contains thujona — not for pregnancy, not for extended use.

plants.common_name_primary: Salvia / Algodoncillo / Cordón de San Francisco
plants.notes: Mexican sage. Valle de México. Used for cough, stomach pain, abortive (S2 explicitly: 'infusión de la planta sola' = abortive). Menstrual disorders, 'aire'. Three 'salvia' plants in MX: S. officinalis (European), S. leucantha (Mexican), S. microphylla (Mexican endemic) — different pharmacology.

Multiple indigenous names across Náhuatl, Popoloca. Bilis, digestive disorders, diuretic, laxative, sedante suave. S2 also: anticonceptive use in some communities. S4: also called 'malva del sapo'. Widespread naturalized in Mexico.

Mexican endemic. Bark — antibiotic, antiseptic, cicatrizant, anti-inflammatory (ovaries). Important in Mexican traditional medicine, particularly Guerrero and Morelos. Commercial bark sold nationally.

Mexican endemic use. Hormonal stimulant, aphrodisiac, fertility enhancement in women and testosterone in men. Also for nervous system, fatigue, stress, menopause. Baja California origin. Cross-dataset: in Bolivia as 'damiana del campo'.

Already documented in Bolivia dataset. Chilean/Argentine native appearing in Mexican herbolaria (imported). Biliary, diuretic, antiparasitic. Do not use for more than 4 weeks; irritates gastric mucosa.

Mexican endemic. Bark isoflavones — diuretic, diarrhea, kidney/urinary infections, weight loss. Famous for fluorescent blue color when dissolved in water (resins). Important commercial herb in Mexico.
Mexican endemic mint. Not to be confused with Melissa officinalis (also called 'toronjil' in Spain and elsewhere). Sedative, menstrual cramps, digestive, insomnia. Jalisco to Veracruz.

Also called 'toronjil' — AMBIGUITY with Agastache mexicana (also 'toronjil'). European lemon balm. Sedative, dismenorrhea, headache, digestive, antidepressant. S4 also: calambres, palpitaciones. Widely used.

plants.common_name_primary: Espinosilla / Chuparrosa / Hierba de la Virgen
plants.notes: Mexican endemic. Small spiny plant. Bilious pain, inapetencia. CEFOFOR: cocimiento suave, 5g per liter. Also in Zacatecas, Querétaro, Chihuahua, Hidalgo.
Root: empacho, diarrhea, burns, muscle pain. Historical antidote name ('contra-hierba'). Morelos, Jalisco, Veracruz, Chiapas.

plants.common_name_primary: Doradilla / Flor de peña / Rosa de Jericó
plants.notes: Resurrection plant. Strong decoction for gout, kidney stones, prostate problems, diuretic. Desert characteristic plant. Distinct from S. convoluta (Yucatan doradilla/much'kok — TRAMIL-validated for kidney stones).

TRAMIL-validated for kidney stones in Caribbean basin. Yucatan: used with elote, e'lemuy. Distinct from S. lepidophylla (desert doradilla) — same common name, different species, similar use for kidney stones but different distribution.

CEFOFOR: postpartum baths, cough, bilious pain, empacho. Historical use as mild intoxicant/hallucinogen (listed as plant with consciousness-altering properties by some sources). Alkaloids present. Native from Texas to Argentina.
Mexican hawthorn. S2: fruit and bark for coronary circulation, tachycardia. S6: C. mexicana variant — bark for diarrhea, tos, leaves for pressure, heart tonic. Both document native Mexican species.

European hawthorn. INPI 2021 compendium: cardiac insufficiency, atherosclerosis, tachycardia, malaria. Distinct from Mexican tejocote (Crataegus pubescens). Both called 'tejocote' or 'espino' in commerce — pharmacological disambiguation needed.
S5 Yucatan (TRAMIL-validated): with guarumbo and árnica for diabetes. S6 Jalisco: diabetes and bilious pain. Náhuatl: nextamalxochitl. Multiple other regional names. Cross-dataset: also in Guatemala.
Valle de México. Mazahua, Otomí indigenous names. Toothache, digestive, postpartum baths. 'Escobilla' name shared with other plants across Latin America.
Náhuatl: azomiatl. Multiple Purépecha and Otomí names. Valle de México: espanto, mal de ojo, cólicos, fiebre, dolor de espalda. Also called 'jaral', 'jarilla amarilla'.

plants.common_name_primary: Poleo de agua / Té de monte
plants.notes: Valle de México. 'Poleo de agua' — creates ambiguity with M.pulegium (also 'poleo'). Excellent digestive, cólicos. Used in elaborate postpartum bath formulas (S2 lists 14+ companion plants).

plants.common_name_primary: Santamaría / Altamiza / Hierba de Santa María
plants.notes: Syn: Tanacetum parthenium. Called 'santamaría', 'altamiza', 'artemisa', 'manzanilla romana' across sources. Abortive, menstrual regulation, migraine, digestive. S4: 'corrección de menstruación, abortivo'. Widely used across all regions.

South American bark — source of quinine. Sold in MX for fever, malaria, wounds. Cross-dataset link: also appears in HN/CR/GT datasets where 'quina' is confused with Quassia amara in some regions. S4 lists cinchonaines and proanthocyanidins.

Yucatan: TRAMIL for cough and cholesterol/triglycerides. Pan-tropical use. Seeds contain annonacin — potentially neurotoxic with prolonged consumption. Leaves traditionally used for cancer (limited clinical evidence).
Yucatan: baths for measles. Seeds toxic (pesticidal). Distinct from A. muricata.

Yucatan: traditional healer documents for cancer, diabetes, cirrhosis (oral with ruda and sábila), skin conditions, intestinal parasites. Well-known multipurpose tree. Neem compounds (azadirachtin) — insecticidal, antiparasitic, antifungal.

TRAMIL-validated for bad breath (oral infusion). Maya Yucatan. Kanchu nub and xpay luch used as companion plants. Cross-dataset: 'pata de vaca' also appears in NI, CR datasets but may refer to different Bauhinia spp.

TRAMIL-validated for mal de pinto (tinea versicolor) — 10g seeds fried in 40ml oil, applied topically. Food colorant (annatto). Widely used throughout tropical Americas. Cross-dataset: also in Bolivia, Guianas, Panama datasets.
Peeling papery bark. Maya: diarrhea (oral), cuts (topical), headache baths. Important Mesoamerican resin tree.

TRAMIL-validated (Yucatan) for intestinal flora regeneration and hemorrhoids (with piña, nopal, kalanchoe, ajo). S6: laxative, antiparasitic (seeds), cosmetic (latex for warts). Seeds mildly toxic in excess. Cross-dataset: also in NI, Honduras, Guatemala datasets.
Yucatan: diabetes (oral with árnica). Same Maya name as Tecoma stans — disambiguation needed. Cecropia contains chlorogenic acid and isoorientin — antihyperglycemic evidence.

plants.scientific_name: Chromolaena odorata (L.) R. M. King & H. Rob.
plants.notes: TRAMIL-validated for kidney stones and kidney problems (with multiple companion plants including corn silk). Also validates use for forúnculos and cutaneous ulcers externally. Invasive weed globally.
TRAMIL-validated for stomach pain and diarrhea. Yucatan. Alkaloids include cissampeline (muscle relaxant). Cross-dataset: also in Guianas dataset.
TRAMIL-validated (Yucatan) for urinary conditions — water of the fruit taken orally. Widely cultivated in coastal Mexico.
TRAMIL-validated for dysentery. Yucatan endemic range. Important timber tree with edible fruit. Bark decoction.
TRAMIL-validated for mal de ojo (baths with chakaj and cruz ak'). Yucatan Peninsula endemic. Called 'ruda de monte' — creates ambiguity with true Ruta chalepensis. Traditional Maya ritual plant.

TRAMIL-validated (Yucatan) for female sterility and male impotence (separate uses). Flowers also for conjunctivitis (S5 TRAMIL table: leaf juice). Widely cultivated ornamental. Distinct from Hibiscus sabdariffa (Flor de Jamaica — beverage).

plants.common_name_primary: Siempreviva / Hoja del aire / Hierba de la bruja
plants.notes: TRAMIL-validated (Yucatan) for carnosidad and conjunctivitis (ophthalmic). S6: diabetes, sore throat, menstrual cramps, headache, diuretic. S1 Zongolica: dysentery. Cross-dataset: called 'hoja de milagro' in NI and 'siempre viva/planta maravilla' in Yucatan. K. pinnata = Bryophyllum pinnatum (synonym confirmed in GT dataset).

TRAMIL-validated (Yucatan) for cold sweats (baths). Leaves used for interdigital fungus and headache (macerado topically). Important food crop. Raw cassava contains cyanogenic glycosides — must be processed for consumption.
TRAMIL-validated for diabetes (oral). Widely commercialized globally. Cross-dataset: also in NI dataset (added from UNAN-León survey). Yucatan cultivation established.
TRAMIL-validated for diabetes. Yucatan/Peninsula endemic range. Thorny shrub.
Sensitive plant — leaves fold when touched. Yucatan: for insomnia. Monoamine oxidase inhibitor properties documented.

ABORTIFACIENT: documented in NI dataset as one of the 'Salvia crisis' abortive plants. Yucatan source lists for 'esterilidad en mujeres, pasmo' — the female sterility use and 'pasmo' (post-trauma condition) context. FloraLexicon must flag: abortifacient properties confirmed in cross-dataset analysis. Distinguish from P. odorata (S2 Valle de MX) which has different properties.

Valle de México. Vaginal lavages, menstrual cólicos, female sterility, rheumatism, GI disorders. Distinct from P. carolinensis (Yucatan) — different uses and safety profile. Both plants share some properties with Pluchea carolinensis which is abortive.
Bark and fruit. Cicatrizant, ovarian inflammation, respiratory diseases, antidiarrheal. CEFOFOR. Multiple indigenous names across Nahua, Maya, Mixtec, Purépecha regions.

Common in dry forests across Mexico. Bark for gastrointestinal disorders, oral infections (gargle). S6 notes: fruit is aphrodisiac; high doses cause constipation. Multiple Náhuatl, Zapotec, Maya regional names.
Common thorny shrub of dry Mexico. Bark/leaves for gastrointestinal, oral antiseptic, diuretic, gastric ulcers. Multiple indigenous names. Used nationally.

Mexican ash. Jalisco, Michoacán, México, Hidalgo, Nayarit. Leaves for fever, rheumatism, antimalarial. Distinct from European Fraxinus excelsior (also medicinal — S4 uses for rheumatism, gout but lists as 'fresno' broadly).

plants.common_name_primary: Grama / Pata de gallo / Bermuda grass
plants.notes: Common lawn grass. CEFOFOR: kidney problems, prostate, uric acid, gout, diuretic (strong decoction 10g/L). Náhuatl: acacahuiztli. Maya: kan-suuk. Widely naturalized.

plants.common_name_primary: Hierba del pollo / Chipil de piedra
plants.notes: Vivid blue flowers distinctive. Valle de México and national. Menstrual cramps, gastric ulcers, antihemorrhagic (S6). S2: hemorragias, heridas, menstrual regulation. Náhuatl: matlalitzic.

S2: seeds as chiquiador for headache; broth for menstrual issues, burns, fertility promotion. S4 (as ejote/bean pod): cellulitis, urinary retention, skin conditions, diabetes (non-insulin dependent). Important food staple with overlooked medicinal uses.

S2: múltiple uses including corn silk (barba) for hepatitis, kidney pain. Root for female sterility, postpartum, avoid abortion. S5 TRAMIL-validated: corn silk for kidney pain, edema, inflammation. Sacred origin — Mexico is the center of domestication. Multiple languages: ixi'im (Maya), nuni (Mixteco), tsíri (Purépecha), ajan (Tzotzil).

Universal medicinal plant. Antiseptic, hypotensive, diuretic, antiparasitic, anticoagulant. S4: rejuvenating properties, arterial restoration. Avoid during lactation (colic in infants). Good gastric tolerability if taken with food.
Gout, bronchitis, anti-inflammatory, empacho, arthritis, anticoagulant (CEFOFOR).
TRAMIL-validated for tinea (ringworm) and skin fungal infections. Yucatan Maya name 'secreto'. Used with altanisa/Parthenium hysterophorus.
MODERATE TOXICITY: cardiac glycosides (as all Asclepias). Yucatan: topical for skin pustules/grains. S5 cautious — only topical use documented. Contains cardenolides.
Yucatan: for diabetes and heart conditions (oral). Night-blooming cactus vine. Edible fruit. Growing commercial importance.

plants.common_name_primary: Ciruela de monte / Jocote / Abal ak' (Maya)
plants.notes: Yucatan: postpartum care and sarpullido (skin rash) baths (with granada, ruda, x k'anan). Topical for scabies. Edible fruit.
Valle de México. Stomach pain (fruit macerated in water), cough/asthma (seeds soaked and eaten), snakebite (seed infusion). Oaxaca: zaandy ujts; San Luis Potosí: bomba kapel (Tenek).

South American native widely cultivated in Mexico for digestive conditions, diarrhea, biliary colic. S2: for 'levantar la mollera' (folk condition in infants), scorpion sting. CEFOFOR: sedante suave, facilitates menstruation.

CONTACT ALLERGEN: causes allergic contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals. S5 Yucatan: used for fungal infections on skin. S2 Valle de México: listed among plants but minimal use noted. Cross-dataset: also in HN dataset. Invasive globally; major agricultural pest.

names.name: Hierbabuena
names.usage_context: general — name shared with MX006, MX007

names.name: Albahaca / Albahacar
names.usage_context: national — also called 'orégano' in some regions

names.name: Cardo mariano / Cardo de María / Cardo lechero
Cardo santo (Jalisco)
confused_with: Argemone mexicana (MX015 — also cardo santo in Zongolica)

names.name: Diente de León / Achicoria amarga / Amargón
Hierba del cáncer
names.usage_context: Zongolica/national — also applies to other plants

names.name: Hierba del golpe (women's use)
names.usage_context: Zongolica — same species, different name and use

names.name: Cempasúchil / Cempazúchil / Cempoalxóchitl
names.usage_context: national — Day of the Dead flower

names.name: Pericón / Yerbanís / Hierba de San Juan
Sangre de Cristo
names.usage_context: national — name shared with other plants in SA

names.name: Insulina / Planta de insulina / Costus ardiente
confused_with: Plectranthus ornatus (MX038 — also called insulina in Zongolica)

names.name: Boldo mexicano / Falso boldo / Buscapina natural
Sauco / Flor y fruto de sauco
Tilo
confused_with: Justicia pectoralis (Tilo in CR/CA — no sedative alkaloids)

names.name: Valeriana mexicana / Raíz del oso / Raíz de gato
Hierba del pollo / Ojo de venado
Maguey morado
confused_with: Tradescantia zebrina (MX067 — also 'maguey' in some areas)

names.name: Palo azul / Palo dulce
names.usage_context: national — famous for blue fluorescence in water

names.name: Doradilla / Flor de peña / Rosa de Jericó
Siempreviva / Hoja del aire / Hierba de la bruja
Hoja de milagro (indirect)
confused_with: Kalanchoe pinnata = Hoja de milagro in NI
Tradescantia zebrina = Hoja de milagro in CR

names.name: Maíz / Elote
names.usage_context: national — Mexico as center of domestication

uses.indication: golpes, contusiones, hematomas, dolor musculoesquelético
antiinflamatorio, antimicrobiano (Staph. aureus, E. coli), antioxidante
uses.clinical_note: INIFAP review: sesquiterpene lactones confirmed antiinflammatory; homeopathic formulations showed positive results in human trials

uses.indication: golpes, esguinces, hematomas, dolor muscular, reumatismo
uses.clinical_note: European pharmacopeia standard; different sesquiterpene profile from MX001

uses.indication: inflamación, heridas, dolor de estómago, reumatismo, diabetes (Yucatan)
parásitos intestinales, empacho, tónico estomacal, afecciones de la matriz
antiparasitario (Trypanosoma cruzi, Plasmodium falciparum, Leishmania amazonensis)
uses.clinical_note: INIFAP: laboratory/animal studies confirmed; ascaridol 300mg/kg convulsive/lethal in mice — DOSE CRITICAL

uses.indication: condimento en frijoles, tamales, quelites
dolor de estómago, digestión, estimulante, diaforético, espasmos intestinales, cólicos
antibacteriano (Gram+), antifúngico (Candida albicans), antioxidante, antiinflamatorio
uses.clinical_note: INIFAP: 228 studies reviewed; GRAS FDA; effective vs S. aureus; anti-inflammatory on pain receptors in rats

uses.indication: quemaduras, heridas, llagas, inflamación intestinal, hemorroides, dolor de cabeza, estreñimiento, varices, problemas de piel, tos, antitumoral
uses.clinical_note: INIFAP: 75+ identified bioactive compounds; promotes cellular proliferation/migration; antifungal, antiseptic, antiviral, antibacterial confirmed

uses.indication: diabetes (con nopal en ayunas)
hepatoprotector, cirrosis, insuficiencia biliar, mala digestión, cálculos renales y vesiculares
uses.clinical_note: Silymarin bioactive; S4 notes properties lost in water — use capsules

uses.indication: problemas oculares, tos, reumatismo (uso externo), sarna, heridas, sedante nervioso
afecciones hepáticas, inflamaciones reumáticas, asma, afecciones cardíacas
diarrea, disentería, parásitos, antiinflamatorio, alopecia, problemas dérmicos, baños postparto
uses.tramil_note: TRAMIL validated: 7 hojas grandes en decocción para diarrea

uses.indication: cáncer (tumores externos e internos), heridas, hinchazones, dolor de estómago, diarrea
regulación menstrual, dolor menstrual, parásitos intestinales, flatulencias, mal de ojo, tónico uterino
purificar sangre, anemia, plaquetas, desórdenes menstruales, dengue, disentería, diabetes, riñones, mal de hígado
diabetes tipo I y II, desórdenes renales, diurético, antioxidante, antimicrobiano, anticancerígeno
uses.clinical_note: INIFAP: 19 animal studies confirm hypoglycemic effect; 1 human clinical study confirms glucose reduction with leaf powder; high potassium, calcium, copper, zinc

uses.indication: diabetes tipo II, reducción de glucosa, protección renal y hepática, obesidad, hipertensión, inflamación, cáncer
uses.clinical_note: INIFAP: rebaudiósido A 242x más dulce que azúcar; estudios clínicos confirman reducción de glucosa en ayuno y postprandial; no teratogénico, mutagénico ni carcinogénico

uses.indication: endulzante natural, agente edulcorante sin calorías
gastritis nerviosa, cólicos infantiles, eczema, insomnio, ansiedad, problemas de piel, diarrea, afecciones oculares
uses.clinical_note: INIFAP: >120 identified compounds; apigenin anti-inflammatory, anxiolytic, anticancer (pre-clinical); GRAS FDA; effective for infant colic in 58% of cases

uses.indication: tos, fiebre, gripe, bronquitis, afecciones pulmonares; descongestión nasal; uso ritual (limpias)
caída de cabello, calentura (uso externo), hemorroides, jiotes, pies hinchados
uses.tramil_note: TRAMIL: hojas externas validadas; semillas NUNCA

uses.indication: enfermedades broncopulmonares, faringitis, laringitis, estreñimiento, sistema gástrico
hemorroides, dolor muscular, articulaciones (EXTERNO ÚNICAMENTE)
uses.preparation: cocimiento para baños de asiento y fomentos

uses.indication: alucinógeno ceremonial, planta espiritual
conjuntivitis (flores blancas, tópico oftálmico ÚNICAMENTE — TRAMIL)
uses.safety_note: TRAMIL validates ONLY ophthalmic use of white flowers; ORAL USE DANGEROUS

uses.indication: diarrea (hojas), candidiasis bucal (látex tópico)
uses.preparation: decocción de hojas oral; látex tópico en boca
uses.tramil_note: TRAMIL validates leaf decoction for diarrhea and latex for oral candidiasis only

uses.indication: insomnio, nerviosismo extremo, presión arterial alta, espasmos digestivos, reumatismo, ansiedad
insomnio, epilepsia, nervios, ansiedad, calambres, dolor muscular, hipertiroidismo
cálculos renales, vesícula biliar, hígado, estreñimiento, cistitis, infecciones urinarias
disentería, diarrea, hemorroides
uses.preparation: hojas maceradas en limón y miel, 3x día

uses.indication: limpieza vaginal, pasmo, tos, retraso menstrual, torceduras, traumatismos
múltiples (cancer, diabetes, HIV/AIDS — community belief)
uses.notes: Evidence level traditional only — scientific evidence lacking for most indications

uses.indication: enfermedades respiratorias, tos, asma, bronquitis, irritación de garganta, catarro
respiratorio, digestivo, piel, úlceras, parásitos, cáncer, ciática
favorecer/estimular contracciones de parto, trastornos menstruales, abortivo, postparto
uses.preparation: cocimiento concentrado, con canela y chocolate

uses.indication: baños postparto, reumas, hemorragia nasal, calambres, fiebre; tumores y apostemas; hipnótico
hipertensión, hemorroides, várices
antibiótico, antiséptico, cicatrizante, astringente, antiinflamatorio de ovarios
diarrea, infecciones urinarias, afecciones renales, adelgazar
cálculos renales, problemas de próstata, gota, diurético
baños postparto, tos, dolor bilioso, empacho
esterilidad en mujeres, pasmo (tratamiento)
uses.safety_note: ABORTIFACIENT — confirmed in cross-dataset analysis (NI dataset). Yucatan use context suggests abortifacient effect possible.

uses.indication: dolor de riñón, edema, inflamación (pelos de elote)
uses.tramil_note: TRAMIL validated: corn silk for kidney pain, edema

safety.contraindications: No usar en niños antes de cirugía sin consulta médica (S3 INIFAP: 84.7% padres dan sin avisar al médico)
safety.notes: No internal toxicity documented; external use safe. Árnica triangle disambiguation critical.

safety.contraindications: Uso externo; no tragar
En exceso: náuseas, vómito, dolor abdominal, convulsiones, parálisis, coma (ascaridol tóxico en altas dosis)
safety.notes: INIFAP: ascaridol a 300mg/kg convulsivo/letal en ratones. Médico toxicólogo MX recomienda no usar para desparasitar. Solo en infusión.

safety.contraindications: Mujeres embarazadas (tujona — provoca menstruación), puede causar convulsiones en dosis altas
Abortivo; no para embarazadas
TÓXICA — protopina y berberina. No exceder 10 días uso oral. Solo látex y baños validados por TRAMIL. No ingerir en embarazo.
TÓXICA (S2)
TÓXICA (S2 — flag explícita)
ABORTIFACIENTE. No en embarazo. Evitar en niños. Uso en infusión máx 10 días.
Safrol — no más de 15 días uso continuo. TRAMIL Nicaragua clasifica como tóxico oral. MX fuentes lo usan ampliamente sin restricción.
Uso prolongado puede irritar mucosa gástrica
SEMILLAS LETALES — ricina. Uso SOLO externo de hojas. NUNCA ingerir semillas.
safety.notes: Collision con higuera/higuera del diablo — nombre peligroso en comercio

safety.contraindications: No documentadas
SOLO USO EXTERNO. Alucinógeno potente. Uso clínico criminal documentado. Tropanos (atropina, escopolamina, hiosciamina). NUNCA uso interno.
Alcaloides vinca — quimioterapéuticos, tóxicos a células normales. NO ingerir. TRAMIL: evitar ingesta oral. S6 (CEFOFOR) recomienda infusión oral — CONTRADICE guía TRAMIL.

confused_with: pine nuts — also called piñón in some contexts
safety.contraindications: SEMILLAS LETALES. Solo látex tópico para candidiasis y decocción de hojas para diarrea validados. JAMÁS semillas.
Cardenólidos cardiotóxicos. SOLO uso externo en preparaciones multi-planta. NUNCA interno.
safety.drug_interactions: glucósidos cardíacos — efectos aditivos potencialmente letales

safety.contraindications: Semillas contienen casimiroína — alcaloide hipnótico potente. Solo carne del fruto segura. Hojas y semillas con precaución.
S4 advierte: puede crear dependencia
ABORTIVA (S1 explícito). No en embarazo. GT dataset: 'no usar más de 15 días, puede manchar el intestino'.
Semillas tóxicas (S4 explícito). Solo flores.
ABORTIFACIENTE EFECTIVO Y PELIGROSO (S2 cita explícita). No usar sin supervisión médica. Contraindicado en embarazo absolutamente.
Solo uso ginecológico/obstétrico especializado
TÓXICA (S6). Frutos potencialmente tóxicos (S2). Solo raíz en cocimiento controlado.
TÓXICA (S6). Solo uso tópico de látex en cantidades muy pequeñas para verrugas y callos. Látex irritante.
Látex ligeramente irritante. No tocar ojos.
Ninguna documentada
Tujona — no en embarazo, no lactancia, no uso prolongado
ABORTIVA (S2 explícito — 'infusión de la planta sola'). No en embarazo.
Ninguna mayor documentada
Alcaloides presentes. Uso moderado. Históricamente documentado como intoxicante leve.
ABORTIVA (S2, S4). No en embarazo. No en lactancia.
ABORTIFACIENTE confirmado en dataset NI. Yucatan context sugiere mismo efecto. No en embarazo.
Dermatitis de contacto alérgica en personas sensibles. Manejo con guantes recomendado.
